content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Margaret McArthur 
Valentine, Inc. 
1213 K street, Ste. 101 
Sacramento, 
Ca 
95814 
Dear Ms. McArthur: 
December 31, 1985 
Re: 
FPPC No. A-85-246 
This is in reply to your letter dated November 27, 1985, and 
to confirm our telephone conversation of today. 
You have asked if an organization which solicited and 
received funds from its members for the purpose of fighting a 
piece of legislation may donate the funds to a political action 
committee, or use the funds to hire a lobbyist. 
The Political Reform Act places no restrictions on the use 
of the funds. 
However, the organization must file disclosure 
reports under the Political Reform Act if it uses the funds for 
campaign purposes or uses them to pay a lobbyist. 
Enclosed for your information are the FPPC's "Information 
Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions" and "Information Manual 
on Lobbying Disclosure Provisions." 
These manuals set out the 
basic disclosure provisions for campaign and lobbying. 
However, 
legislative changes which go into effect January 1, 1986, will 
result in changes to both the campaign and the lobbying disclosure 
provisions. 
Therefore, please contact our office in late January 
for the revised information manuals. 
Also enclosed for your 
information are interim fact sheets which explain the 1986 
provisions. 
In our telephone conversation, you indicated that the 
organization may create its own political action committee and use 
the funds already collected as start up money. 
So long as the 
payments made by the members to the organization were not 
"earmarked" for campaign purposes (that is, at the time the 
members made the payments they did not know or have reason to know 
that the money would be used for campaign purposes), the funds 
will be a "contribution" from the organization to its PAC. 
Of 
course, any payments made by the members in the future must be 
reported as contributions from the members to the PAC. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Margaret A. McArthur 
December 31, 1985 
Page 2 
Since the organization is unsure at this time what use will 
be made of the funds, I suggest that you or another representative 
of the organization contact us if the funds are used for campaign 
or lobbying purposes. 
We can then provide specific information 
regarding the reporting requirements. 
JP:kt 
Sincerely, 
Jeanne Pritchard 
Chief, Technical Assistance 
& Analysis Division 


Ms. Barbara Mi Iman 
General Counsel 
FaIr Political Practices Commfsslon 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CalifornIa 95804 
Dear Ms. Mf Iman: 
Dennis 1. Valentine 
November 21, 1985 
On behalf of a client, we are writing for an answer to the 
follo\<dng: 
Members of a society were solicited for funds to fight a piece 
of 
egi5latlon. 
Can the society contribute 
hese funds or part 
of these funds direct I y to the PAC and/or use them to hire a 
lobbyist? 
We would appreciate an answer as soon as possible. 
Sincerely, 
Margar 
A. McArthur 
VALENTINE, INCORPORATED 
M Mis 
"

sections.question: Members of a society were solicited for funds to fight a piece of legislation. Can the society contribute these funds or part of these funds directly to the PAC and/or use them to hire a lobbyist?
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act places no restrictions on the use of the funds. However, the organization must file disclosure reports under the Political Reform Act if it uses the funds for campaign purposes or uses them to pay a lobbyist. So long as the payments made by the members to the organization were not 'earmarked' for campaign purposes, the funds will be a 'contribution' from the organization to its PAC.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether an organization may donate funds solicited from members for fighting legislation to a political action committee or use them to hire a lobbyist.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The organization may donate funds to a PAC or hire a lobbyist without legal restrictions, but must file appropriate disclosure reports if funds are used for campaign or lobbying purposes. Member payments not earmarked for campaign purposes when made qualify as organizational contributions to a PAC.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (November 21, 1985 letter from Valentine, Inc.) and FPPC response (December 31, 1985 letter from Chief Pritchard). Structured as advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Legislative changes effective January 1, 1986 mentioned as context for disclosure requirements.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Members of a society were solicited for funds to fight a piece of legislation. Can the society contribute these funds or part of these funds directly to the PAC and/or use them to hire a lobbyist?

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act places no restrictions on the use of the funds. However, the organization must file disclosure reports under the Political Reform Act if it uses the funds for campaign purposes or uses them to pay a lobbyist. So long as the payments made by the members to the organization were not 'earmarked' for campaign purposes, the funds will be a 'contribution' from the organization to its PAC.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that an organization may donate member-solicited funds to a political action committee or use them to hire a lobbyist without restrictions on use, but must file required disclosure reports under the Political Reform Act depending on the funds' ultimate use.
